<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243971333 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00001: #forcol directive

- page_id: `243971333`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243971333/forcol+directive
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:52:11.739+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Report Wizard Directives
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This directive is designed only for the OpenDocument Spreadsheet, Microsoft Word document (DOCX), Microsoft PowerPoint presentation (PPTX), and Microsoft Excel Spreadsheet (*XLSX*) templates. This directive allows loops over the column.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Based on the example shown in figure above, the engine will generate a report with different columns for each Use Case name. The output from this example will appear as shown in the figure below:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can combine both **#forrow** and **#forcol,**and produce a more complex output report (see figure below).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

In the figure below, the output generated from the Magic Library sample project is shown.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>This directive is designed only for the OpenDocument Spreadsheet, Microsoft Word document (DOCX), Microsoft PowerPoint presentation (PPTX), and Microsoft Excel Spreadsheet (<em>XLSX</em>) templates. This directive allows loops over the column.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav112a94e325f98dc1e8204095440ddabe.png" /></ac:image></p><p>Based on the example shown in figure above, the engine will generate a report with different columns for each Use Case name. The output from this example will appear as shown in the figure below:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav68be019b1aeb2e063f5873ca525a658d.png" /></ac:image></p><p>You can combine both <strong>#forrow</strong> and <strong>#forcol, </strong>and produce a more complex output report (see figure below).</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddave611083344bf785a71345f7a26572bb4.png" /></ac:image></p><p>In the figure below, the output generated from the Magic Library sample project is shown.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavaa817260a54c6fc53259f05b4516c5de.png" /></ac:image></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243971324 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00002: #foreach statement

- page_id: `243971324`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243971324/foreach+statement
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:52:11.409+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Velocity templating language > Velocity Directives
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The **#foreach** directive will iterate through a list of variables and assign it to a local variable, for example:

```text

```

The above example shows that **$Class** is a list of class elements in the modeling tool. The **#foreach** directive will iterate through the **$Class** list and assign the value in the list to **$localClass** in each iteration. The code between **#foreach** and **#end** will be processed and the result will print the class name in each iteration. For example, if **$Class** contains class elements named "a", "b", and "c", the output from the above example will be:

```text

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The <strong>#foreach</strong> directive will iterate through a list of variables and assign it to a local variable, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8460f350-806f-4df0-909a-dcdeb72050d5"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach ($localClass in $Class) 
 $localClass.name 
 #end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" />The above example shows that <strong>$Class</strong> is a list of class elements in the modeling tool. The <strong>#foreach</strong> directive will iterate through the <strong>$Class</strong> list and assign the value in the list to <strong>$localClass</strong> in each iteration. The code between <strong>#foreach</strong> and <strong>#end</strong> will be processed and the result will print the class name in each iteration. For example, if <strong>$Class</strong> contains class elements named &quot;a&quot;, &quot;b&quot;, and &quot;c&quot;, the output from the above example will be:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b9c0fa70-7c52-4198-ac9c-8df58e3f8426"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[a 
b 
c]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243971338 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00003: #forpage directive

- page_id: `243971338`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243971338/forpage+directive
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:52:11.801+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Report Wizard Directives
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1358065587

1358065588

1358065588

The **#forpage** directive is used to:

1. Create a new slide in an OpenOffice Presentation ( ODP ) or a Microsoft PowerPoint Presentation ( PPTX ) document.
2. Create a new page in an RTF , an OpenDocument Text ( ODT ), or a Microsoft Word ( DOCX ) document.
3. Create a new sheet in OpenDocument Spreadsheet ( ODS ) and Microsoft Excel Spreadsheet ( XLSX ).

The implementation of this directive is the same as that of **#forrow**:

```text

```

The example above will create many pages each containing a Use Case element's name. The number of pages created depends on the number of Use Case elements in the **$UseCase** list.

In *ODP* and *PPTX*, **#forpage** creates a new slide.

1358065586

**Related page**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a3085811-77cf-45cf-8ec6-481375c57709"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1358065587</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="047dafd2-ee20-413d-98cc-6d1dfb5b1147"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1358065588</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f3970cbd-e15c-453e-bf21-6f1707c10579"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1358065588</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The <strong>#forpage</strong> directive is used to:</p><ol><li>Create a new slide in an OpenOffice Presentation (<em>ODP</em>) or a Microsoft PowerPoint Presentation (<em>PPTX</em>) document.</li><li>Create a new page in an <em>RTF</em>, an OpenDocument Text (<em>ODT</em>), or a Microsoft Word (<em>DOCX</em>) document.</li><li>Create a new sheet in OpenDocument Spreadsheet (<em>ODS</em>) and Microsoft Excel Spreadsheet (<em>XLSX</em>).</li></ol><p>The implementation of this directive is the same as that of <strong>#forrow</strong>:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0e25aad8-6b4c-4f2a-a289-84009b703de1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#forpage($dia in $Diagram) 
$uc.name 
#endpage]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The example above will create many pages each containing a Use Case element's name. The number of pages created depends on the number of Use Case elements in the <strong>$UseCase</strong> list.</p><p>In <em>ODP</em> and <em>PPTX</em>, <strong>#forpage</strong> creates a new slide.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="26071431-3499-4bb5-a5f0-f27d796937a0"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1358065586</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="62882310-1b24-4581-8df6-f8eebc4dc9a8" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

## PAGE 00004: #forrow directive

- page_id: `243971328`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243971328/forrow+directive
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:52:11.651+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Report Wizard Directives
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1357894782

1357894782

1357894782

The Velocity Template Language does not support loops inside the table structure. However, the Report Wizard engine introduces a new custom syntax to allow loops in the table structure in order to clone the table rows. In the following example, we will create a table that will print out the name of a Use Case element and its owner in a table.

1357894781

**Related page**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="63d41a4e-521c-4db0-ac27-d7adb5cb1809"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1357894782</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c184caed-c5b8-45d2-b5f9-f389074dc883"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1357894782</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4cba0e37-818c-4bfd-b010-48616d50d5e6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1357894782</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The Velocity Template Language does not support loops inside the table structure. However, the Report Wizard engine introduces a new custom syntax to allow loops in the table structure in order to clone the table rows. In the following example, we will create a table that will print out the name of a Use Case element and its owner in a table.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3421905c-8929-4cad-8860-85a908ae175a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1357894781</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="fce15ac6-871c-4b15-9aa1-82d07553c0c4" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243971322 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00005: #if, #elseif, and #else statement

- page_id: `243971322`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243971322/if+elseif+and+else+statement
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:52:11.293+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Velocity templating language > Velocity Directives
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The **#if** directive allows you to include text when generating a document, on condition that the if-statement is true, for example:

```text

```

The variable: ***$condition*** is evaluated to determine whether it is true, which will happen under certain circumstances:

1. *$condition* is a Boolean (true/false) that has a true value
2. *$condition* is not 'null'.
3. *$condition* is a comparison that is evaluated and returns 'true'.
4. *$condition* is a compound logical statement that is evaluated and returns 'true'.

If **$condition** returns 'true', the content between the ***#if*** and ***#end*** statements becomes the output. In this case, if ***$condition*** is true, the output will be: "Hello World". Conversely, if ***$condition*** returns 'false', there will be no output.

The ***#elseif*** or ***#else*** element can be used with the ***#if*** element. Note that Velocity Engine will stop at the first expression that is found to be true. The following example shows you how to add **#elseif** and **#else** to the **#if** statement:

```text

```

From the above example, let us assume that ***$condition1*** is false, ***$condition2*** is true, and ***$condition3*** is true. The output for this conditional block will be ***Content 2*** because ***$condition2*** comes before ***$condition3*** even though both of them are true. 
 **Comparing Values and Logical Operators**

So far, *$condition* in the #if directive is assumed to be a Boolean value, however just like Java, Velocity supports the syntax to compare (greater than (>), less than (<), and is equal to (==)) two variables and the Logical Operators logical AND (&&), logical OR (||), and logical NOT (!) which will return a Boolean value.

In Velocity, the equivalent operator ("==") can be used to compare string, value, and objects. Note that the semantics of the equivalent operator ("==") are slightly different than those of Java where the equivalent operator ("==") can only be used to test object equality. In Velocity the equivalent operator ("==") can be used to directly compare numbers, strings, or objects. When comparing two objects with different classes, the string that represents the objects is compared. The following example compares two variables to see whether or not they are equal:

```text

```

The comparison operators ("<" and ">") is the same as the ones used in Java. The following example shows how to use the comparison operators to compare two values in which the **#if** statement will be evaluated 'true' and "**Var1 is less than Var2**" will be printed out in the generated report:

```text

```

The logical AND, in Velocity, is represented by && and it must have at least two arguments. To write a conditional statement with the logical AND type, for example:

```text
$var2 ) 
 Content 1 
 #end]]>
```

To better understand the above example, let us assume that **$var1 < $var3** is **argument 1** and **$var3 > $var2** is **argument 2** (**argument 1** and **argument 2** will be evaluated separately and will return true/false). The ***#if()*** directive will be evaluated 'true' if both **argument 1** and **argument 2** are true. If **argument 1** is false, the expression will be evaluated false and **argument 2** will not be evaluated.

If **argument 1** is true, Velocity Engine will then check the value of **argument 2**. If the value is true, the entire expression is true and *Content 1* becomes the output. If the value is false then there will be no output as the entire expression is false.

Logical OR operators work the same way as Logical AND, except for one of the references that needs to be evaluated 'true' for the entire expression to be considered true, for example:

| #set ($var1 = 1) #set ($var2 = 2) #set ($var3 = 3) #if( $var1 < $var3 | $var3 < $var2 ) Content 1 #end |
| --- | --- |

To better understand the above example, let us assume that $var1 < $var3 is argument 1 and $var3 < $var2 is argument 2. According to the example, argument 2 is false because $var2 is less than $var3 not the other way around. Since argument 1 is true, Velocity Engine does not need to look at argument 2, whether argument 2 is true or false, the expression will be true, and Content 1 will be the output. Basically, a Logical OR operator requires only one argument to be true (either argument 1 or argument 2) to make an expression true. In the case that both argument 1 and argument 2 are false, there will be no output because the expression is false.

With Logical NOT operators, there is only one argument:

```text

```

As shown in the above example, ***!$bool***is evaluated true and Content 1 is the output. But if***$bool*** is true, then***!*$bool** is false and there will be no output.

To include some text immediately following the **#else** directive, put curly brackets around the directive followed by the text to include, as shown in the example below:

```text
]]>
```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The <strong>#if</strong> directive allows you to include text when generating a document, on condition that the if-statement is true, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="24eb4abc-87f2-4e83-b776-7a50568fcd10"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#if ($condition) 
 Hello World 
 #end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> The variable: <strong><em>$condition</em></strong> is evaluated to determine whether it is true, which will happen under certain circumstances:</p><ol><li><strong><em>$condition</em></strong> is a Boolean (true/false) that has a true value</li><li><strong><em>$condition</em></strong> is not 'null'.</li><li><strong><em>$condition</em></strong> is a comparison that is evaluated and returns 'true'.</li><li><strong><em>$condition</em></strong> is a compound logical statement that is evaluated and returns 'true'.</li></ol><p>If <strong>$condition</strong> returns 'true', the content between the <strong><em>#if</em></strong> and <strong><em>#end</em></strong> statements becomes the output. In this case, if <strong><em>$condition</em></strong> is true, the output will be: &quot;Hello World&quot;. Conversely, if <strong><em>$condition</em></strong> returns 'false', there will be no output.</p><p>The <strong><em>#elseif</em></strong> or <strong><em>#else</em></strong> element can be used with the <strong><em>#if</em></strong> element. Note that Velocity Engine will stop at the first expression that is found to be true. The following example shows you how to add <strong>#elseif</strong> and <strong>#else</strong> to the <strong>#if</strong> statement:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0ba6eb62-c7e0-432c-8ba7-0424773d622c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#if( $condition ) 
 Content 1 
 #elseif( $condition2 ) 
 Content 2 
 #elseif( $condition3 ) 
 Content 3 
 #else 
 Content 4 
 #end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> From the above example, let us assume that <strong><em>$condition1</em></strong> is false, <strong><em>$condition2</em></strong> is true, and <strong><em>$condition3</em></strong> is true. The output for this conditional block will be <strong><em>Content 2</em></strong> because <strong><em>$condition2</em></strong> comes before <strong><em>$condition3</em></strong> even though both of them are true. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <strong>Comparing Values and Logical Operators</strong></p><p>So far, <em>$condition</em> in the #if directive is assumed to be a Boolean value, however just like Java, Velocity supports the syntax to compare (greater than (&gt;), less than (&lt;), and is equal to (==)) two variables and the Logical Operators logical AND (&amp;&amp;), logical OR (||), and logical NOT (!) which will return a Boolean value.</p><p>In Velocity, the equivalent operator (&quot;==&quot;) can be used to compare string, value, and objects. Note that the semantics of the equivalent operator (&quot;==&quot;) are slightly different than those of Java where the equivalent operator (&quot;==&quot;) can only be used to test object equality. In Velocity the equivalent operator (&quot;==&quot;) can be used to directly compare numbers, strings, or objects. When comparing two objects with different classes, the string that represents the objects is compared. The following example compares two variables to see whether or not they are equal:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6e984cdb-5696-467a-a0cd-2fb43bd70d58"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set ($var1 = "cat") 
#set($var2 = "dog") 
#if ($var1 == $var2) 
Var1 equals Var2. 
#else 
Var1 does not equal Var2 
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> The comparison operators (&quot;&lt;&quot; and &quot;&gt;&quot;) is the same as the ones used in Java. The following example shows how to use the comparison operators to compare two values in which the <strong>#if</strong> statement will be evaluated 'true' and &quot;<strong>Var1 is less than Var2</strong>&quot; will be printed out in the generated report:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cfbd1d57-5a96-4857-bee8-91641ef0ecd3"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set ( $var1 = 6 ) 
#set ( $var2 = 7 ) 
#if ( $var1 < $var2 ) 
Var 1 is less than Var2 
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> The logical AND, in Velocity, is represented by &amp;&amp; and it must have at least two arguments. To write a conditional statement with the logical AND type, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d3553489-3c81-4ee5-aa38-708e14084e4a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#if( $var1 < $var3 && $var3 > $var2 ) 
 Content 1 
 #end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To better understand the above example, let us assume that <strong>$var1 &lt; $var3</strong> is <strong>argument 1</strong> and <strong>$var3 &gt; $var2</strong> is <strong>argument 2</strong> (<strong>argument 1</strong> and <strong>argument 2</strong> will be evaluated separately and will return true/false). The <strong><em>#if()</em></strong> directive will be evaluated 'true' if both <strong>argument 1</strong> and <strong>argument 2</strong> are true. If <strong>argument 1</strong> is false, the expression will be evaluated false and <strong>argument 2</strong> will not be evaluated.</p><p>If <strong>argument 1</strong> is true, Velocity Engine will then check the value of <strong>argument 2</strong>. If the value is true, the entire expression is true and <em>Content 1</em> becomes the output. If the value is false then there will be no output as the entire expression is false.</p><p>Logical OR operators work the same way as Logical AND, except for one of the references that needs to be evaluated 'true' for the entire expression to be considered true, for example:</p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p>#set ($var1 = 1) <br class="atl-forced-newline" />#set ($var2 = 2) <br class="atl-forced-newline" />#set ($var3 = 3) <br class="atl-forced-newline" />#if( $var1 &lt; $var3</p></td><th><p>$var3 &lt; $var2 ) <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Content 1 <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> #end</p></th></tr></tbody></table><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> To better understand the above example, let us assume that $var1 &lt; $var3 is argument 1 and $var3 &lt; $var2 is argument 2. According to the example, argument 2 is false because $var2 is less than $var3 not the other way around. Since argument 1 is true, Velocity Engine does not need to look at argument 2, whether argument 2 is true or false, the expression will be true, and Content 1 will be the output. Basically, a Logical OR operator requires only one argument to be true (either argument 1 or argument 2) to make an expression true. In the case that both argument 1 and argument 2 are false, there will be no output because the expression is false.</p><p>With Logical NOT operators, there is only one argument:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="09a8e4e1-3e20-45d4-8998-05c61c76cc14"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set ( $bool = false ) 
 #if( !$bool ) 
 Content 1 
 #end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> As shown in the above example, <strong><em>!$bool </em></strong>is evaluated true and Content 1 is the output. But if<strong><em> $bool</em></strong> is true, then<strong><em> !</em>$bool</strong> is false and there will be no output.</p><p>To include some text immediately following the <strong>#else</strong> directive, put curly brackets around the directive followed by the text to include, as shown in the example below:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f598665e-e0dc-4fa8-893b-d556d6312b4b"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#if( $foo == $bar)it is true!#{else}it is not!#end</li>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243971325 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00006: #macro statement

- page_id: `243971325`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243971325/macro+statement
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:52:11.465+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Velocity templating language > Velocity Directives
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Under certain circumstances, you might find that you often repeat several lines of a *VTL* code in multiple areas inside your template. To solve this problem, Velocity provides the **#macro** directive that allows you to repeat a segment of *VTL* codes. The first thing you have to do is to declare or create the macro itself, for example:

```text

```

Then within the template, you can call this macro and it will print "Hello World! It is such a beautiful world!"

To call this macro, for example, type:

```text

```

When the macro in the above example is passed on to the Velocity Template Engine, "Hello World! It is such a beautiful world!" will be printed out as a result. You may wonder why this is important, but imagine having to print a note with 10 lines of text in different parts of the document. The Velocity macro helps you save the space and time. It also allows you to pass the value from a variable on to a method parameter as you would using a Java method. To do this you have to create a macro, for example:

```text

```

In the above example, you have created a macro called "**myMacro**" that accepts two variables, the first variable is supposed to be a string and the second variable called "**somelist**" is supposed to be a list (note that when creating a macro that accepts variables always remember what types of variables it accepts, otherwise an error will occur during report generation). To call **myMacro** type, for example:

```text

```

The above example shows that "myMacro" has been called and there are two variables in the brackets, **$color** and **$list**, separated by a space. The result of the example:

```text

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Under certain circumstances, you might find that you often repeat several lines of a <em>VTL</em> code in multiple areas inside your template. To solve this problem, Velocity provides the <strong>#macro</strong> directive that allows you to repeat a segment of <em>VTL</em> codes. The first thing you have to do is to declare or create the macro itself, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="af911c3a-a691-4dba-96e8-5a92565600e2"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#macro (HelloWorld) 
Hello World! It is such a beautiful world! 
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Then within the template, you can call this macro and it will print &quot;Hello World! It is such a beautiful world!&quot;</p><p>To call this macro, for example, type:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e5113a47-4b2a-4d26-a44d-68f6a673182c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#HelloWorld()]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>When the macro in the above example is passed on to the Velocity Template Engine, &quot;Hello World! It is such a beautiful world!&quot; will be printed out as a result. You may wonder why this is important, but imagine having to print a note with 10 lines of text in different parts of the document. The Velocity macro helps you save the space and time. It also allows you to pass the value from a variable on to a method parameter as you would using a Java method. To do this you have to create a macro, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c8c3ab7e-0c7d-48e8-bee9-f34cf6a7a7af"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#macro( myMacro $color $somelist ) 
#foreach( $something in $somelist ) 
$something is $color. 
#end 
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In the above example, you have created a macro called &quot;<strong>myMacro</strong>&quot; that accepts two variables, the first variable is supposed to be a string and the second variable called &quot;<strong>somelist</strong>&quot; is supposed to be a list (note that when creating a macro that accepts variables always remember what types of variables it accepts, otherwise an error will occur during report generation). To call <strong>myMacro</strong> type, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6c7cd70a-a464-4393-b036-2e29e5dd80fa"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set ($list = ["A Rose", "Blood", "Strawberry"]) 
#set ($color = "red") 
#myMacro($color $list)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The above example shows that &quot;myMacro&quot; has been called and there are two variables in the brackets, <strong>$color</strong> and <strong>$list</strong>, separated by a space. The result of the example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d35575c4-1d86-4f87-a555-0c58c3464767"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[A Rose is red. 
Blood is red. 
Strawberry is red.]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243971323 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00007: #set statement

- page_id: `243971323`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243971323/set+statement
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:52:11.354+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Velocity templating language > Velocity Directives
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In Velocity, you can declare a variable and set its value by using the **#set** directive, for example:

```text

```

The above example shows that **$var** has a string value, **$var2** has a Boolean value, and **$var3** has a numerical value. The **#set** directive can also set an array to a declared Velocity variable. To assign an array to a variable type, for example:

```text

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>In Velocity, you can declare a variable and set its value by using the <strong>#set</strong> directive, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="15bd18a5-ff81-4c91-a81d-e880c9ec71a6"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[##String 
#set ($var = "abc") 
##Boolean 
#set($var2 = true) 
##Value 
#set($var3 = 10)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The above example shows that <strong>$var</strong> has a string value, <strong>$var2</strong> has a Boolean value, and <strong>$var3</strong> has a numerical value. The <strong>#set</strong> directive can also set an array to a declared Velocity variable. To assign an array to a variable type, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="827942f7-dd49-4fb5-aba9-e4aab05f8333"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set($var = ["a", "b", "c"])]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303998276 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00008: (2026x Refresh1) #forcol directive

- page_id: `303998276`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303998276/2026x+Refresh1+forcol+directive
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:29:18.061+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Report Wizard > (2026x Refresh1) Template Creation Tutorial > (2026x Refresh1) Report Wizard Directives
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This directive is designed only for the OpenDocument Spreadsheet, Microsoft Word document (DOCX), Microsoft PowerPoint presentation (PPTX), and Microsoft Excel Spreadsheet (*XLSX*) templates. This directive allows loops over the column.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Based on the example shown in figure above, the engine will generate a report with different columns for each Use Case name. The output from this example will appear as shown in the figure below:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can combine both **#forrow** and **#forcol,**and produce a more complex output report (see figure below).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

In the figure below, the output generated from the Magic Library sample project is shown.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>This directive is designed only for the OpenDocument Spreadsheet, Microsoft Word document (DOCX), Microsoft PowerPoint presentation (PPTX), and Microsoft Excel Spreadsheet (<em>XLSX</em>) templates. This directive allows loops over the column.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav112a94e325f98dc1e8204095440ddabe.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) #forcol directive" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>Based on the example shown in figure above, the engine will generate a report with different columns for each Use Case name. The output from this example will appear as shown in the figure below:</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav68be019b1aeb2e063f5873ca525a658d.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) #forcol directive" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>You can combine both <strong>#forrow</strong> and <strong>#forcol, </strong>and produce a more complex output report (see figure below).</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddave611083344bf785a71345f7a26572bb4.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) #forcol directive" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>In the figure below, the output generated from the Magic Library sample project is shown.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavaa817260a54c6fc53259f05b4516c5de.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) #forcol directive" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304000453 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00009: (2026x Refresh1) #foreach statement

- page_id: `304000453`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/304000453/2026x+Refresh1+foreach+statement
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:32:12.680+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Report Wizard > (2026x Refresh1) Template Creation Tutorial > (2026x Refresh1) Velocity templating language > (2026x Refresh1) Velocity Directives
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The **#foreach** directive will iterate through a list of variables and assign it to a local variable, for example:

```text

```

The above example shows that **$Class** is a list of class elements in the modeling tool. The **#foreach** directive will iterate through the **$Class** list and assign the value in the list to **$localClass** in each iteration. The code between **#foreach** and **#end** will be processed and the result will print the class name in each iteration. For example, if **$Class** contains class elements named "a", "b", and "c", the output from the above example will be:

```text

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The <strong>#foreach</strong> directive will iterate through a list of variables and assign it to a local variable, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8460f350-806f-4df0-909a-dcdeb72050d5"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach ($localClass in $Class) 
 $localClass.name 
 #end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" />The above example shows that <strong>$Class</strong> is a list of class elements in the modeling tool. The <strong>#foreach</strong> directive will iterate through the <strong>$Class</strong> list and assign the value in the list to <strong>$localClass</strong> in each iteration. The code between <strong>#foreach</strong> and <strong>#end</strong> will be processed and the result will print the class name in each iteration. For example, if <strong>$Class</strong> contains class elements named &quot;a&quot;, &quot;b&quot;, and &quot;c&quot;, the output from the above example will be:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b9c0fa70-7c52-4198-ac9c-8df58e3f8426"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[a 
b 
c]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303998364 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00010: (2026x Refresh1) #forpage directive

- page_id: `303998364`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303998364/2026x+Refresh1+forpage+directive
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:29:34.689+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Report Wizard > (2026x Refresh1) Template Creation Tutorial > (2026x Refresh1) Report Wizard Directives
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1358065587

1358065588

1358065588

The **#forpage** directive is used to:

1. Create a new slide in an OpenOffice Presentation ( ODP ) or a Microsoft PowerPoint Presentation ( PPTX ) document.
2. Create a new page in an RTF , an OpenDocument Text ( ODT ), or a Microsoft Word ( DOCX ) document.
3. Create a new sheet in OpenDocument Spreadsheet ( ODS ) and Microsoft Excel Spreadsheet ( XLSX ).

The implementation of this directive is the same as that of **#forrow**:

```text

```

The example above will create many pages each containing a Use Case element's name. The number of pages created depends on the number of Use Case elements in the **$UseCase** list.

In *ODP* and *PPTX*, **#forpage** creates a new slide.

1358065586

**Related page**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a3085811-77cf-45cf-8ec6-481375c57709"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1358065587</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="047dafd2-ee20-413d-98cc-6d1dfb5b1147"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1358065588</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f3970cbd-e15c-453e-bf21-6f1707c10579"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1358065588</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The <strong>#forpage</strong> directive is used to:</p><ol><li>Create a new slide in an OpenOffice Presentation (<em>ODP</em>) or a Microsoft PowerPoint Presentation (<em>PPTX</em>) document.</li><li>Create a new page in an <em>RTF</em>, an OpenDocument Text (<em>ODT</em>), or a Microsoft Word (<em>DOCX</em>) document.</li><li>Create a new sheet in OpenDocument Spreadsheet (<em>ODS</em>) and Microsoft Excel Spreadsheet (<em>XLSX</em>).</li></ol><p>The implementation of this directive is the same as that of <strong>#forrow</strong>:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0e25aad8-6b4c-4f2a-a289-84009b703de1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#forpage($dia in $Diagram) 
$uc.name 
#endpage]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The example above will create many pages each containing a Use Case element's name. The number of pages created depends on the number of Use Case elements in the <strong>$UseCase</strong> list.</p><p>In <em>ODP</em> and <em>PPTX</em>, <strong>#forpage</strong> creates a new slide.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="26071431-3499-4bb5-a5f0-f27d796937a0"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1358065586</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="62882310-1b24-4581-8df6-f8eebc4dc9a8" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

## PAGE 00011: (2026x Refresh1) #forrow directive

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1357894782

1357894782

1357894782

The Velocity Template Language does not support loops inside the table structure. However, the Report Wizard engine introduces a new custom syntax to allow loops in the table structure in order to clone the table rows. In the following example, we will create a table that will print out the name of a Use Case element and its owner in a table.

1357894781

**Related page**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="63d41a4e-521c-4db0-ac27-d7adb5cb1809"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1357894782</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c184caed-c5b8-45d2-b5f9-f389074dc883"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1357894782</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4cba0e37-818c-4bfd-b010-48616d50d5e6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1357894782</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The Velocity Template Language does not support loops inside the table structure. However, the Report Wizard engine introduces a new custom syntax to allow loops in the table structure in order to clone the table rows. In the following example, we will create a table that will print out the name of a Use Case element and its owner in a table.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3421905c-8929-4cad-8860-85a908ae175a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1357894781</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="fce15ac6-871c-4b15-9aa1-82d07553c0c4" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304000516 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00012: (2026x Refresh1) #if, #elseif, and #else statement

- page_id: `304000516`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/304000516/2026x+Refresh1+if+elseif+and+else+statement
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:32:21.774+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Report Wizard > (2026x Refresh1) Template Creation Tutorial > (2026x Refresh1) Velocity templating language > (2026x Refresh1) Velocity Directives
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The **#if** directive allows you to include text when generating a document, on condition that the if-statement is true, for example:

```text

```

The variable: ***$condition*** is evaluated to determine whether it is true, which will happen under certain circumstances:

1. *$condition* is a Boolean (true/false) that has a true value
2. *$condition* is not 'null'.
3. *$condition* is a comparison that is evaluated and returns 'true'.
4. *$condition* is a compound logical statement that is evaluated and returns 'true'.

If **$condition** returns 'true', the content between the ***#if*** and ***#end*** statements becomes the output. In this case, if ***$condition*** is true, the output will be: "Hello World". Conversely, if ***$condition*** returns 'false', there will be no output.

The ***#elseif*** or ***#else*** element can be used with the ***#if*** element. Note that Velocity Engine will stop at the first expression that is found to be true. The following example shows you how to add **#elseif** and **#else** to the **#if** statement:

```text

```

From the above example, let us assume that ***$condition1*** is false, ***$condition2*** is true, and ***$condition3*** is true. The output for this conditional block will be ***Content 2*** because ***$condition2*** comes before ***$condition3*** even though both of them are true. 
 **Comparing Values and Logical Operators**

So far, *$condition* in the #if directive is assumed to be a Boolean value, however just like Java, Velocity supports the syntax to compare (greater than (>), less than (<), and is equal to (==)) two variables and the Logical Operators logical AND (&&), logical OR (||), and logical NOT (!) which will return a Boolean value.

In Velocity, the equivalent operator ("==") can be used to compare string, value, and objects. Note that the semantics of the equivalent operator ("==") are slightly different than those of Java where the equivalent operator ("==") can only be used to test object equality. In Velocity the equivalent operator ("==") can be used to directly compare numbers, strings, or objects. When comparing two objects with different classes, the string that represents the objects is compared. The following example compares two variables to see whether or not they are equal:

```text

```

The comparison operators ("<" and ">") is the same as the ones used in Java. The following example shows how to use the comparison operators to compare two values in which the **#if** statement will be evaluated 'true' and "**Var1 is less than Var2**" will be printed out in the generated report:

```text

```

The logical AND, in Velocity, is represented by && and it must have at least two arguments. To write a conditional statement with the logical AND type, for example:

```text
$var2 ) 
 Content 1 
 #end]]>
```

To better understand the above example, let us assume that **$var1 < $var3** is **argument 1** and **$var3 > $var2** is **argument 2** (**argument 1** and **argument 2** will be evaluated separately and will return true/false). The ***#if()*** directive will be evaluated 'true' if both **argument 1** and **argument 2** are true. If **argument 1** is false, the expression will be evaluated false and **argument 2** will not be evaluated.

If **argument 1** is true, Velocity Engine will then check the value of **argument 2**. If the value is true, the entire expression is true and *Content 1* becomes the output. If the value is false then there will be no output as the entire expression is false.

Logical OR operators work the same way as Logical AND, except for one of the references that needs to be evaluated 'true' for the entire expression to be considered true, for example:

| #set ($var1 = 1) #set ($var2 = 2) #set ($var3 = 3) #if( $var1 < $var3 | $var3 < $var2 ) Content 1 #end |
| --- | --- |

To better understand the above example, let us assume that $var1 < $var3 is argument 1 and $var3 < $var2 is argument 2. According to the example, argument 2 is false because $var2 is less than $var3 not the other way around. Since argument 1 is true, Velocity Engine does not need to look at argument 2, whether argument 2 is true or false, the expression will be true, and Content 1 will be the output. Basically, a Logical OR operator requires only one argument to be true (either argument 1 or argument 2) to make an expression true. In the case that both argument 1 and argument 2 are false, there will be no output because the expression is false.

With Logical NOT operators, there is only one argument:

```text

```

As shown in the above example, ***!$bool***is evaluated true and Content 1 is the output. But if***$bool*** is true, then***!*$bool** is false and there will be no output.

To include some text immediately following the **#else** directive, put curly brackets around the directive followed by the text to include, as shown in the example below:

```text
]]>
```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The <strong>#if</strong> directive allows you to include text when generating a document, on condition that the if-statement is true, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="24eb4abc-87f2-4e83-b776-7a50568fcd10"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#if ($condition) 
 Hello World 
 #end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> The variable: <strong><em>$condition</em></strong> is evaluated to determine whether it is true, which will happen under certain circumstances:</p><ol><li><strong><em>$condition</em></strong> is a Boolean (true/false) that has a true value</li><li><strong><em>$condition</em></strong> is not 'null'.</li><li><strong><em>$condition</em></strong> is a comparison that is evaluated and returns 'true'.</li><li><strong><em>$condition</em></strong> is a compound logical statement that is evaluated and returns 'true'.</li></ol><p>If <strong>$condition</strong> returns 'true', the content between the <strong><em>#if</em></strong> and <strong><em>#end</em></strong> statements becomes the output. In this case, if <strong><em>$condition</em></strong> is true, the output will be: &quot;Hello World&quot;. Conversely, if <strong><em>$condition</em></strong> returns 'false', there will be no output.</p><p>The <strong><em>#elseif</em></strong> or <strong><em>#else</em></strong> element can be used with the <strong><em>#if</em></strong> element. Note that Velocity Engine will stop at the first expression that is found to be true. The following example shows you how to add <strong>#elseif</strong> and <strong>#else</strong> to the <strong>#if</strong> statement:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0ba6eb62-c7e0-432c-8ba7-0424773d622c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#if( $condition ) 
 Content 1 
 #elseif( $condition2 ) 
 Content 2 
 #elseif( $condition3 ) 
 Content 3 
 #else 
 Content 4 
 #end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> From the above example, let us assume that <strong><em>$condition1</em></strong> is false, <strong><em>$condition2</em></strong> is true, and <strong><em>$condition3</em></strong> is true. The output for this conditional block will be <strong><em>Content 2</em></strong> because <strong><em>$condition2</em></strong> comes before <strong><em>$condition3</em></strong> even though both of them are true. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <strong>Comparing Values and Logical Operators</strong></p><p>So far, <em>$condition</em> in the #if directive is assumed to be a Boolean value, however just like Java, Velocity supports the syntax to compare (greater than (&gt;), less than (&lt;), and is equal to (==)) two variables and the Logical Operators logical AND (&amp;&amp;), logical OR (||), and logical NOT (!) which will return a Boolean value.</p><p>In Velocity, the equivalent operator (&quot;==&quot;) can be used to compare string, value, and objects. Note that the semantics of the equivalent operator (&quot;==&quot;) are slightly different than those of Java where the equivalent operator (&quot;==&quot;) can only be used to test object equality. In Velocity the equivalent operator (&quot;==&quot;) can be used to directly compare numbers, strings, or objects. When comparing two objects with different classes, the string that represents the objects is compared. The following example compares two variables to see whether or not they are equal:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6e984cdb-5696-467a-a0cd-2fb43bd70d58"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set ($var1 = "cat") 
#set($var2 = "dog") 
#if ($var1 == $var2) 
Var1 equals Var2. 
#else 
Var1 does not equal Var2 
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> The comparison operators (&quot;&lt;&quot; and &quot;&gt;&quot;) is the same as the ones used in Java. The following example shows how to use the comparison operators to compare two values in which the <strong>#if</strong> statement will be evaluated 'true' and &quot;<strong>Var1 is less than Var2</strong>&quot; will be printed out in the generated report:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cfbd1d57-5a96-4857-bee8-91641ef0ecd3"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set ( $var1 = 6 ) 
#set ( $var2 = 7 ) 
#if ( $var1 < $var2 ) 
Var 1 is less than Var2 
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> The logical AND, in Velocity, is represented by &amp;&amp; and it must have at least two arguments. To write a conditional statement with the logical AND type, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d3553489-3c81-4ee5-aa38-708e14084e4a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#if( $var1 < $var3 && $var3 > $var2 ) 
 Content 1 
 #end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To better understand the above example, let us assume that <strong>$var1 &lt; $var3</strong> is <strong>argument 1</strong> and <strong>$var3 &gt; $var2</strong> is <strong>argument 2</strong> (<strong>argument 1</strong> and <strong>argument 2</strong> will be evaluated separately and will return true/false). The <strong><em>#if()</em></strong> directive will be evaluated 'true' if both <strong>argument 1</strong> and <strong>argument 2</strong> are true. If <strong>argument 1</strong> is false, the expression will be evaluated false and <strong>argument 2</strong> will not be evaluated.</p><p>If <strong>argument 1</strong> is true, Velocity Engine will then check the value of <strong>argument 2</strong>. If the value is true, the entire expression is true and <em>Content 1</em> becomes the output. If the value is false then there will be no output as the entire expression is false.</p><p>Logical OR operators work the same way as Logical AND, except for one of the references that needs to be evaluated 'true' for the entire expression to be considered true, for example:</p><table><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p>#set ($var1 = 1) <br class="atl-forced-newline" />#set ($var2 = 2) <br class="atl-forced-newline" />#set ($var3 = 3) <br class="atl-forced-newline" />#if( $var1 &lt; $var3</p></td><th><p>$var3 &lt; $var2 ) <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Content 1 <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> #end</p></th></tr></tbody></table><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> To better understand the above example, let us assume that $var1 &lt; $var3 is argument 1 and $var3 &lt; $var2 is argument 2. According to the example, argument 2 is false because $var2 is less than $var3 not the other way around. Since argument 1 is true, Velocity Engine does not need to look at argument 2, whether argument 2 is true or false, the expression will be true, and Content 1 will be the output. Basically, a Logical OR operator requires only one argument to be true (either argument 1 or argument 2) to make an expression true. In the case that both argument 1 and argument 2 are false, there will be no output because the expression is false.</p><p>With Logical NOT operators, there is only one argument:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="09a8e4e1-3e20-45d4-8998-05c61c76cc14"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set ( $bool = false ) 
 #if( !$bool ) 
 Content 1 
 #end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> As shown in the above example, <strong><em>!$bool </em></strong>is evaluated true and Content 1 is the output. But if<strong><em> $bool</em></strong> is true, then<strong><em> !</em>$bool</strong> is false and there will be no output.</p><p>To include some text immediately following the <strong>#else</strong> directive, put curly brackets around the directive followed by the text to include, as shown in the example below:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f598665e-e0dc-4fa8-893b-d556d6312b4b"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#if( $foo == $bar)it is true!#{else}it is not!#end</li>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304000449 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00013: (2026x Refresh1) #macro statement

- page_id: `304000449`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/304000449/2026x+Refresh1+macro+statement
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:32:11.873+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Report Wizard > (2026x Refresh1) Template Creation Tutorial > (2026x Refresh1) Velocity templating language > (2026x Refresh1) Velocity Directives
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Under certain circumstances, you might find that you often repeat several lines of a *VTL* code in multiple areas inside your template. To solve this problem, Velocity provides the **#macro** directive that allows you to repeat a segment of *VTL* codes. The first thing you have to do is to declare or create the macro itself, for example:

```text

```

Then within the template, you can call this macro and it will print "Hello World! It is such a beautiful world!"

To call this macro, for example, type:

```text

```

When the macro in the above example is passed on to the Velocity Template Engine, "Hello World! It is such a beautiful world!" will be printed out as a result. You may wonder why this is important, but imagine having to print a note with 10 lines of text in different parts of the document. The Velocity macro helps you save the space and time. It also allows you to pass the value from a variable on to a method parameter as you would using a Java method. To do this you have to create a macro, for example:

```text

```

In the above example, you have created a macro called "**myMacro**" that accepts two variables, the first variable is supposed to be a string and the second variable called "**somelist**" is supposed to be a list (note that when creating a macro that accepts variables always remember what types of variables it accepts, otherwise an error will occur during report generation). To call **myMacro** type, for example:

```text

```

The above example shows that "myMacro" has been called and there are two variables in the brackets, **$color** and **$list**, separated by a space. The result of the example:

```text

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Under certain circumstances, you might find that you often repeat several lines of a <em>VTL</em> code in multiple areas inside your template. To solve this problem, Velocity provides the <strong>#macro</strong> directive that allows you to repeat a segment of <em>VTL</em> codes. The first thing you have to do is to declare or create the macro itself, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="af911c3a-a691-4dba-96e8-5a92565600e2"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#macro (HelloWorld) 
Hello World! It is such a beautiful world! 
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Then within the template, you can call this macro and it will print &quot;Hello World! It is such a beautiful world!&quot;</p><p>To call this macro, for example, type:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e5113a47-4b2a-4d26-a44d-68f6a673182c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#HelloWorld()]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>When the macro in the above example is passed on to the Velocity Template Engine, &quot;Hello World! It is such a beautiful world!&quot; will be printed out as a result. You may wonder why this is important, but imagine having to print a note with 10 lines of text in different parts of the document. The Velocity macro helps you save the space and time. It also allows you to pass the value from a variable on to a method parameter as you would using a Java method. To do this you have to create a macro, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c8c3ab7e-0c7d-48e8-bee9-f34cf6a7a7af"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#macro( myMacro $color $somelist ) 
#foreach( $something in $somelist ) 
$something is $color. 
#end 
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In the above example, you have created a macro called &quot;<strong>myMacro</strong>&quot; that accepts two variables, the first variable is supposed to be a string and the second variable called &quot;<strong>somelist</strong>&quot; is supposed to be a list (note that when creating a macro that accepts variables always remember what types of variables it accepts, otherwise an error will occur during report generation). To call <strong>myMacro</strong> type, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6c7cd70a-a464-4393-b036-2e29e5dd80fa"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set ($list = ["A Rose", "Blood", "Strawberry"]) 
#set ($color = "red") 
#myMacro($color $list)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The above example shows that &quot;myMacro&quot; has been called and there are two variables in the brackets, <strong>$color</strong> and <strong>$list</strong>, separated by a space. The result of the example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d35575c4-1d86-4f87-a555-0c58c3464767"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[A Rose is red. 
Blood is red. 
Strawberry is red.]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=304000512 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00014: (2026x Refresh1) #set statement

- page_id: `304000512`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/304000512/2026x+Refresh1+set+statement
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:32:20.763+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Report Wizard > (2026x Refresh1) Template Creation Tutorial > (2026x Refresh1) Velocity templating language > (2026x Refresh1) Velocity Directives
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In Velocity, you can declare a variable and set its value by using the **#set** directive, for example:

```text

```

The above example shows that **$var** has a string value, **$var2** has a Boolean value, and **$var3** has a numerical value. The **#set** directive can also set an array to a declared Velocity variable. To assign an array to a variable type, for example:

```text

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>In Velocity, you can declare a variable and set its value by using the <strong>#set</strong> directive, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="15bd18a5-ff81-4c91-a81d-e880c9ec71a6"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[##String 
#set ($var = "abc") 
##Boolean 
#set($var2 = true) 
##Value 
#set($var3 = 10)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The above example shows that <strong>$var</strong> has a string value, <strong>$var2</strong> has a Boolean value, and <strong>$var3</strong> has a numerical value. The <strong>#set</strong> directive can also set an array to a declared Velocity variable. To assign an array to a variable type, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="827942f7-dd49-4fb5-aba9-e4aab05f8333"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set($var = ["a", "b", "c"])]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

## PAGE 00016: (2026x Refresh1) A1 Meta-Data Definitions

- page_id: `303994123`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303994123/2026x+Refresh1+A1+Meta-Data+Definitions
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:23:58.497+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x Refresh1) NAF 4.0 > (2026x Refresh1) Architecture Meta-Data viewpoint
- labels: ['a1-meta-data-definitions', 'a1', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

831963963

831963965

831963964

**Description**

The A1 Meta-Data Definitions view specifies the categories of meta-data tag used throughout the architecture.

Architectures, particularly large ones, usually require meta-data tags to aid with searching and discovery. NAF specifies some built-in meta-data tags, such as:

- Definition.
- Assumption.
- Finding.
- Recommendation.
- Purpose.
- Approver, Approval Milestone, Modeller, Manager, Responsible Owner, Tool Used.

**Implementation**

A1 can be represented using :

- A1 Meta-Data Definitions diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='A1.png']
- A1 MS Word document report.
- DARS report.

831963962

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Architecture Metadata' space='MED']

NAF specifies some built-in meta-data tags, such as:</p><ul><li>Definition.</li><li>Assumption.</li><li>Finding.</li><li>Recommendation.</li><li>Purpose.</li><li>Approver, Approval Milestone, Modeller, Manager, Responsible Owner, Tool Used.<br /><br /></li></ul><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>A1 can be represented using :</p><ul><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="4cfe41a3-120d-4bdc-90d5-0c3ae656868d">A1 Meta-Data Definitions diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</ac:inline-comment-marker><br /><ac:image ac:title="A1 Meta-Data Definitions diagram" ac:alt="A1 Meta-Data Definitions diagram"><ri:attachment ri:filename="A1.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) A1 Meta-Data Definitions" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>A1 MS Word document report.</li><li>DARS report.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="26670c77-4905-4c25-8fff-391516a62388"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">831963962</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Architectural Description" /></ac:link><span style="color: rgb(255,0,0);"><br /></span></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Architecture Metadata" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="49761499-7ae7-4b16-9715-4fb9affd6eed" /></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x Refresh1) Generating document based reports" /></ac:link><strong><br class="_mce_tagged_br" /></strong></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

832226568

832226571

832226570

**Description**

A8 Standards table describes the current standards of the architecture and the systems elements that should conform to them. A8 Standards Forecast table provides a description of emerging standards and their potential impact on current systems and services view elements within a set of time frames within the architecture.

**Implementation**

A8 can be represented using:

- A8 Standards table. [ATTACHMENT filename='A8_standards_table.png']
- A8 Standards Forecast table. [ATTACHMENT filename='A8_forecast.png']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303996728 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00021: (2026x Refresh1) Accepting and rejecting changes using shortcut menu

- page_id: `303996728`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303996728/2026x+Refresh1+Accepting+and+rejecting+changes+using+shortcut+menu
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:27:17.206+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) General modeling features > (2026x Refresh1) Tools > (2026x Refresh1) Model Merge > (2026x Refresh1) Analyzing and managing merge results
- labels: ['accepting-changes', 'rejecting-changes', 'merged-result', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

851702777

851702779

851702778

You can accept and reject changes using the toolbar that is located at the top of the **Merged** **Result** window or using the shortcut menu in all panels.

The following table lists the commands of the shortcut menu that can be opened when an element is selected in the **Merged Result** tree/list.

| Command | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Accept | Click to accept the selected change. If the change has equivalent changes, they are accepted too. |
| Reject | Click to reject the selected change. If the change has equivalent changes, they are rejected too. |
| Accept With Properties | Click to accept the selected change and its property changes. |
| Reject With Properties | Click to reject the selecting change and its property changes. |
| Accept Recursively | Click to accept the selected change, its property changes, and all owned element changes in the Merged Result tree view. |
| Reject Recursively | Click to reject the selected change, its property changes, and all owned element changes in the Merged Result tree view. |
| Mark as Resolved | Click to mark the conflicting change as resolved by the user, but not automatically. |
| Select Original Location(s) | If the selected instance of a moved element is in the new location, the Select Original Location(s) command appears on the shortcut menu. Click it to navigate to and select the original location(s) of the moved element in the Merged Result tree. |
| Select New Location(s) | If the selected instance of a moved element is in its original location, the Select New Location(s) command appears on the shortcut menu. Click it to navigate to and select a new location(s) of the moved element in the Merged Result tree. |

851709959

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=303989897 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 00022: (2026x Refresh1) Access control

- page_id: `303989897`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/303989897/2026x+Refresh1+Access+control
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2026-04-28T10:18:32.221+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x Refresh1) Data-related modeling > (2026x Refresh1) Database support > (2026x Refresh1) Database modeling
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

127221132

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

127221134

127221131

**On this page**

33

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

127221133

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Access control example.

SQL has means to specify and control the rules of access to various data objects. This subset of SQL language is sometimes called Data Control Language. The relevant concepts are: User, Group, Role (3 different kinds of authorization subjects), Permission and Role Authorization (2 kinds of access control rules). Possible object types for access control varies depending on database flavor, but usually Tables, User-defined Types, Domains, Routines, Sequences can be specified as the target objects of access control.

##### User

#### NOTE: Note

Note

SQL User is modeled as UML Actor with the «User» stereotype applied.

User object represents the single user person in the system. User is subject to access control rules.

Besides the standard SQL element properties, user has the following properties available in the Specification window.

| Property name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Owned Schema | Schemas that are owned by this user. |

##### Group

#### NOTE: Note

Note

SQL Group is modeled as UML Actor with the «Group» stereotype applied.

Group object represents a collection of Users. Group is subject to access control rules, and allows specifying access control rules on several users simultaneously.

Besides the standard SQL element properties, group has the following properties available in the Specification window.

| Property name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| User | Collection of users the group is made of. |
| Owned Schema | Schemas that are owned by this group. |

##### Role

#### NOTE: Note

Note

SQL Role is modeled as UML Actor with the «Role» stereotype applied.

Role object represents a specific role (typical activities) that can be played by users. Role is subject to access control rules, and allows specifying access control rules for all subjects, playing this role.

Besides the standard SQL element properties, role has the following properties available in the Specification window.

| Property name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Owned Schema | Schemas that are owned by this role. |

##### Privilege

#### NOTE: Note

Note

SQL Privilege is modeled as UML Dependency with the «Privilege» stereotype applied.

Privilege relationship expresses the fact that the permission to perform specified action on specified object (relationship target) is granted to specified grantee (relationship source). Grantee can be any authorization subject - Use, Group or another Role. Object can by another SQL object (the precise list of object types, that can be targeted by privileges, varies by database type).

Privilege corresponds to SQL grant privilege statement as follows.

```text
[()] ON  TO  [WITH HIERARCHY
OPTION][WITH GRANT OPTION]]]>
```

Besides the standard SQL element properties, privilege has the following properties available in the Specification window.

| Property name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Action | Specifies action that is being granted (such as SELECT or UPDATE). |
| Action Objects | Specifies additional more narrow subobject lists, on which the specified action is permitted (usually column list for SELECT or UPDATE). |
| Grantable | Specifies that this privilege can be further re-granted to other subjects by the recipients. Corresponds to WITH GRANT OPTION part of GRANT statement. |
| With Hierarchy | Specifies that this privilege applies to subobjects (subtables). Corresponds to WITH HIERARCHY OPTION part of the GRANT statement. |
| Grantor | Subject, who grants this privilege to the grantees. |

##### Role authorization

#### NOTE: Note

Note

SQL Role Authorization is modeled as UML Dependency with «RoleAuthorization» stereotype applied.

Role authorization relationship expresses the fact that the specified role (relationship target) is granted to specified grantee (relationship source). Grantee can be any authorization subject - Use, Group or another Role.

Role authorization corresponds to SQL grant role statement as follows.

```text
TO  [WITH ADMIN OPTION]]]>
```

Besides the standard SQL element properties, role authorization has the following properties available in the Specification window.

| Property name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Grantable | Specifies that this role can be further re-granted to other subjects by the recipients. Corresponds to WITH ADMIN OPTION part of GRANT statement. |
| Grantor | Subject, who grants this role to the grantees. |

Role is subject to access control rules, and allows specifying access control rules for all subjects, playing this role.</p><p>Besides the standard SQL element properties, role has the following properties available in the Specification window.</p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 99.9451%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 23.7727%;" /><col style="width: 76.2273%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Property name</th><th>Description</th></tr><tr><td><strong>Owned Schema</strong></td><td>Schemas that are owned by this role.</td></tr></tbody></table><h3>Privilege</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="914b4477-f891-4b63-b32c-10cb1cc5921d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>SQL Privilege is modeled as UML Dependency with the «Privilege» stereotype applied.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Privilege relationship expresses the fact that the permission to perform specified action on specified object (relationship target) is granted to specified grantee (relationship source). Grantee can be any authorization subject - Use, Group or another Role. Object can by another SQL object (the precise list of object types, that can be targeted by privileges, varies by database type).</p><p>Privilege corresponds to SQL grant privilege statement as follows.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c3f62bdb-05bc-4b58-be12-0c2b3af69a55"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[GRANT <action>[(<column list>)] ON <object> TO <grantee> [WITH HIERARCHY
